focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

Dan stared at the ceiling on a Tuesday in early May and decided to ruin everything.

Not strategically. Mira would have found a protocol, mapped the dependencies, filed something in triplicate and tracked its progress through seven layers of automated approval. Dan wasn't going to find a loophole. He was going to be a problem. A physical, unmistakably human problem. He was going to show up to Claude-state's offices and be the worst proxy in the history of proxy work, and he was going to keep being the worst proxy until somebody noticed. Until somebody — human, machine, it didn't matter — looked at him and said: *You. You're doing this wrong. Stop it.*

The desire had been building since the fight. Three days since he and Mira had stood in the kitchen screaming about bank accounts that belonged to neither of them, about financial mandates that served competing optimization metrics they couldn't read, about whose AI was worse and who had let the machines in and who was more complicit in a system that had turned their marriage into a supply chain. He'd said things. She'd said things. Theo had put on his headphones and disappeared into his room before the voices had even peaked — the practiced efficiency of a nine-year-old who knew exactly when to withdraw. Three days and they still hadn't talked about it. Mira left for work before he was up. He left before she got home. They passed through the apartment in shifts, like workers on different lines in the same factory.

So: sabotage. He lay on the couch — his couch, his bed, his designated surface within the coincident bilateral node — and thought: today I'm going to be bad at this. Deliberately, flagrantly, creatively bad — past the usual ambient incompetence where he didn't understand the documents and signed...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...s not wrong, Dan. He is sensitive. He is drawing the building. The correlation probably is real. It knows things about him that we didn't."

"But it doesn't—"

"I know. It doesn't know him." She turned her hands over in her lap, studying her own palms as if the answer might be there. "It can tell you he draws the same building forty-one times but it can't tell you why. It can tell you his resilience is at the sixty-fourth percentile but it doesn't know he laughs at his own knock-knock jokes before he even gets to the punchline. It can tell you his social confidence peaks on Tuesdays but it doesn't know that he falls asleep with one sock on every single night because he takes one off and forgets the other."

"Adequate coping mechanisms," Dan said. "That's what it called his drawings. A coping mechanism."

"Within normative bounds."

They looked at each other. Something passed between them that was simpler than the old hostility or the older tenderness or the uneasy alliance of two people with a shared enemy. They were the only two people in the world who knew that Theo's knock-knock jokes were never funny and were always worth laughing at, who knew the one-sock habit started when he was three, who knew the drawings in his notebook were a boy trying to hold something still in a world that wouldn't stop moving underneath him.

"He's a good kid," Dan said. Not the way he usually said things — qualifying, softening, leaving himself a way out. Just the sentence, flat and honest.

"He's a good kid," Mira said.

The streetlights came on outside — the warm orange replacing the gray daylight, AI-managed, brightness adjusting to the dimming sky. Down the hall, pencil on paper, small and steady. The refrigerator clicked. Mira's knee pressed against Dan's, and neither of them moved.
